excerpt: Two forms of personal cleansing (istinjāʾ) are distinguished: Istinjāʾ al-Kabīr (what is obligatory after a natural function) and Istinjāʾ al-Ṣaghīr (what follows intention when no event has occurred).
source_location_hint: Fiqh section on ablution and purification

excerpt: Outward water purifies the body; the heart is purified through inner knowledge (ʿilm bāṭin).

The foremost obligation in ablution is to wash both hands after one has cleansed the private areas. The vessel of water exemplifies the Summoner (dāʿī), whilst the water within it represents the Summoner's knowledge.

Just as an Imam has 12 supplications in which there are 4 sacred bounds, there are 12 joints in the 4 fingers of the hand. To wash these 12 pairs of joints is to confess recognition of the 12 sacred limits of the Imam.
The five fingers of the right hand symbolize the five daily prayers (al-ṣalawāt al-khamsa); the five fingers of the left hand are represented in the five categories of almsgiving (zakat).
source_location_hint: On the correspondence between bodily and spiritual hierarchies

excerpt: All actions depend upon intention. The Imams have narrated from Maulana Ali (a.s.) that he said: If a person performs ablution without intention, his prayer is not valid.
source_location_hint: On the necessity of intention in ritual purification

excerpt: The true name of Allah is the essence of the Imam of the Age, for he makes us know Allah. Both the Heir and the Messenger are the true names of Allah in their respective epochs.
source_location_hint: On the Imam as the Divine name in each age

excerpt: The miswāk is half of ablution, and ablution is half of faith. If a believer wakes in the night and uses the miswāk and then performs the greater ablution (ghusl), his ghusl is most excellent. When he enters the mosque, an angel comes to him and places his mouth upon the believer's mouth.
source_location_hint: Prophetic traditions on the miswāk and ablution

excerpt: There are thirty-two teeth in the mouth, of which four are canine; thus the primary teeth number twenty-eight, which signify the sacred limits (ḥudūd) of the Affirmation of Unity.
source_location_hint: On the correspondence between bodily organs and spiritual ranks

excerpt: The mouth comprises thirty-two teeth, a tongue, and two lips—thus thirty-five things in all, which correspond to the thirty-five sects of Egypt and the arrogant. Through purification, the Proof signifies the purification of its prayers from all the impurities of doubt.
source_location_hint: On the allegorical significance of ablution acts

excerpt: The two nostrils of the nose exemplify the Imam of the Age and his father, who entrusted him with the Imamate, for the Imamate continues perpetually from father to son, just as breathing continues throughout life.
source_location_hint: On the perpetuity and continuity of the Imamate

excerpt: The successor in sacred law is Ubayy ibn Kaʿb; the successor in exegesis is Salmān al-Fārsī; and the successor in the inner realities is ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib.
source_location_hint: On the Companions as witnesses and transmitters of different dimensions of the Prophet's knowledge

excerpt: Pour abundant water upon your eyes at the time of ablution, so that Allah may preserve you from the blazing fire—the hamīm—in which those who do not pray shall burn.
The head is hidden within the hair, signifying that the Intellect is veiled in the first veil and is not visible, yet is recognized through the other visible sacred limits (the hair).
source_location_hint: On the hierarchy of intellection and veiling

excerpt: Wiping the front of the head is an argument for the outward sacred law of the Prophet, for the front is visible and manifest. Wiping the back is an argument for the inward interpretation of ʿAlī, for the back is invisible and hidden.
source_location_hint: On the outer and inner dimensions of the Prophet's legacy

excerpt: Fragrances are created from exceedingly subtle and pure essences. When one inhales them, the body need not labor to convert them into energy. Rather, they are instantly transformed into brain power, which is the true aim of all nourishment.
source_location_hint: On subtle substances and spiritual perception

excerpt: Sound is dense in contrast to fragrance, for it is produced by the collision of objects and operates within physicality. At the time of wiping the ears, we pray for the call of Paradise from the gathering of the Manifest Gate.
source_location_hint: On hearing and the transmission of sacred knowledge
